title: WLC 123-133: The Fifth Commandment — Honor Your Father and Mother (Part I)
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy

outline.title: Transition: WLC Q122 — The Sum of the Last Six Commandments
subpoints: Commandments 1-4 address duty to God (the vertical); commandments 5-10 address duty to man (the horizontal)
Jesus summarizes: love the Lord your God, and love your neighbor as yourself
The Ten Commandments guide us in what it means to love our neighbors — not just kindness but the full scope of the commandments

outline.title: The Text of the Fifth Commandment (WLC Q123)
subpoints: "Honor your father and your mother, that your days may be long upon the land which the LORD your God is giving you"
Two versions: Exodus 20:12 and Deuteronomy 5:16 — Deuteronomy adds "as the LORD your God commanded you" and "that it may be well with you"
"As the LORD commanded you" emphasizes this is God's law, not parents' self-interest
"That it may be well with you" emphasizes quality of life, not only length of life
Paul calls this "the first commandment with a promise" (Ephesians 6:2-3)

outline.title: What Does It Mean to Honor? (Acronym: DAD)
subpoints: Hebrew word for honor (kavod) is related to the word for glory — a weightiness, gravity, and reverence
Honor is not primarily "obey" — it is broader: a disposition of the heart
D — Disposition: respect (because God appointed them), thankfulness, willingness to help and serve
A — Actions: for young children, obedience; for everyone, attentiveness, care for aging parents
D — Diction (Speech): speaking respectfully to parents and speaking well of parents to others
Jesus rebuked the Pharisees for using the Corban tradition to dishonor parents (Matthew 15:4)

outline.title: Does Honor Always Mean Obey?
subpoints: For children in the home: yes — Paul in Ephesians 6:1 says "Children, obey your parents in the Lord"
For adult children: no longer obligated to obey in the same way, but still bound to honor
Genesis 2:24: "a man shall leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife" — the leave-and-cleave principle establishes a new household authority
Married daughters are to be under the authority of their husbands, not primarily their fathers
Exceptions to children obeying parents: when asked to sin; to preserve life; where parental intent clearly would permit an exception
The inner child's question: "Any other exceptions?" — check your disposition, not the loopholes
Parents' obligations: make it easy for children to honor you; hold children accountable to the commandment

summary:
Elder Andrew Hoy teaches the first of three lessons on the fifth commandment — "Honor your father and your mother" — using Westminster Larger Catechism questions 122 through 133. He begins with WLC Q122, which frames commandments 5-10 as the summary of our duty to our neighbor, before moving to Q123 and the text of the fifth commandment itself. He notes the differences between the Exodus and Deuteronomy versions, finding significance in Deuteronomy's additions: the reminder that this is God's direct command, and the expansion of the promise to include not just longevity but quality of life.

The heart of the lesson is an exploration of what it means to honor, organized around the acronym DAD: Disposition (a heart attitude of respect, gratitude, and willingness to serve), Actions (obedience for children in the home, attentiveness and care for adult children), and Diction (speaking respectfully to and about one's parents). Elder Hoy emphasizes that honor is broader than mere obedience — it is more like the disposition of reverence one shows to a highly esteemed person, flowing naturally from a right understanding of God's appointment of one's parents.

The lesson also addresses the question of whether children must always obey their parents. For children in the home, obedience is indeed required (Ephesians 6:1), but the leave-and-cleave principle of Genesis 2:24 establishes that married adult children are no longer under parental authority in the same way. Exceptions to the obedience requirement are narrow: when asked to sin, or possibly in extreme situations to preserve life. The lesson closes with a challenge to both children (check your disposition, not the loopholes) and parents (make it easy for your children to honor you, and hold them accountable to the commandment).
transcript:
## Introduction

As elders we have a great joy in participating in new member interviews and children's interviews. In those children's interviews, there is always a moment when the other elders turn to look at me — because invariably when we ask, "What are some examples of sins?" every child answers, "Hitting and kicking my brothers and sisters." I then ask whether they understand sin is more than that, probing to see if they understand the commandment we're addressing this morning: to honor their father and mother.

Today we have the first of three lessons on the fifth commandment, using the Westminster Larger Catechism as our guide. You might ask why three weeks for this commandment. After all, most of us in this room are not children still living at home with our parents. But you likely know that this commandment applies to adult children as well. What may be less known is that the Reformed tradition has not read the fifth commandment as applying exclusively to the parent-child relationship — it applies to any authority relationship.

Questions 123-133 of the Larger Catechism:
- Q123: the text of the commandment
- Q124-126: the intended audience and scope of responsibilities
- Q127-128: duties of inferiors to superiors
- Q129-130: duties of superiors to inferiors
- Q131-133: duties of equals; and the promise attached

This morning I will focus primarily on the text of the commandment itself (Q123), with applications mainly to the parent-child relationship. Next week will bring the broader applications. But first, a transition question:

## WLC Q122: The Sum of the Last Six Commandments

Q122 asks: "What is the sum of the six commandments which contain our duty to man?" Answer: "The sum of the six commandments which contain our duty to man is, to love our neighbor as ourselves, and to do to others what we would have them do to us."

Commandments 1-4 address our vertical relationship — how man is to relate to God, how man is to love God. Commandments 5-10 address our horizontal orientation — how we are to love our neighbor. This picks up on Jesus in Luke 10, who responded to the great commandment question with two parts: love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength; and love your neighbor as yourself.

Do we naturally think of the Ten Commandments when asked what it means to love our neighbor? Many of us would first think of kindness, patience, generosity — all good expressions of love. But we also need to remember that commandments 5-10 guide us in loving our neighbors. The Larger Catechism helps us see the fullness of what these commandments contain. My hope as we move through this material is that we would not only understand God's word better but grow in our love for God and for our neighbors.

## WLC Q123: The Text of the Fifth Commandment

Q123: "Which is the fifth commandment?" Answer: "The fifth commandment is, Honor thy father and thy mother, that thy days may be long upon the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee."

We find the commandment in both Exodus 20 and Deuteronomy 5. There are differences between the two renderings. Both begin with "Honor your father and your mother." But Deuteronomy 5 adds "as the LORD your God has commanded you." It also adds "that it may be well with you" after "that your days may be long."

What do we make of these additions? The first — "as the LORD your God has commanded you" — feels almost redundant, since the preface already established these as God's commands. But Moses in Deuteronomy seems to want to emphasize that this is a law from God, not merely from parents. This would be an easy command to overlook — easy to forget how important it is to honor our father and mother. The emphasis reminds us: this is not about what parents want; it is what God requires.

The second addition — "that it may be well with you" — adds quality to the promise of long life. Yes, long life is good. But Moses adds: it will be well with you in the land. A long life filled with blessing; a life in which things go well.

Paul in Ephesians 6:2-3 calls this "the first commandment with a promise" — both the length and the quality of blessing attached to obedience.

## What Does It Mean to Honor?

The commandment says "honor" — not "obey." When many of us hear this commandment, we hear "obey." But honor is broader than obedience. What is the meaning of this word?

The Hebrew word translated honor (kavod) is the same word translated elsewhere as "glory." (Thanks to Elder Hollander for confirming this.) It carries a sense of weightiness, gravity, and significance. In application, to honor someone is to treat them with the reverence and esteem that is due them.

To help us think about honoring our parents, I offer the acronym **DAD**:

**D — Disposition.** Honor begins in the heart. This is not first about external conformity. Dennis Prager has argued that the Ten Commandments are purely about external behavior — that the commandment against adultery isn't about lust in the heart (directly contradicting Jesus in Matthew 5). If we follow Prager's logic, honoring parents would be only about outward actions. But Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount shows how to read the law: it addresses the heart. The fifth commandment begins with a disposition — an attitude of respect and reverence toward one's parents.

What does a disposition of honor look like?
- Respect: not primarily because your parents are respectable, but because God appointed them to you. Whatever you think of your parents, they are the parents God in his infinite wisdom chose to give you. Your respect for them begins with the God who gave them to you.
- Thankfulness: for their sacrifices, their love, all they have done and continue to do.
- Willingness: a cheerful readiness to serve them, whether in the home or in caring for them in old age.

**A — Actions.** A true honoring disposition must manifest in actions — you cannot say "I have a deeply respectful heart toward my parents" if it never shows. For young children, this manifests primarily in obedience. For everyone, it manifests in attentiveness — even adult children should remain attentive to their parents' lives, not cutting them off when independence is achieved. Care for aging parents is also in view here: are you willing to serve them sacrificially when they need you?

(I'll confess — part of the reason we have six children is to increase the odds that one of them will care for us in old age. I hope at least one of them marries someone with a large property where they can build us a nice little house among the grandchildren. That's not entirely a joke.)

**D — Diction (Speech).** This is perhaps the most important and most neglected category for adult children. The way we speak to our parents — with respect and reverence. The way we speak about our parents — casting them in a good light to others. A useful test: if someone who had never met your parents heard only what you have said about them, would they walk away with a high view of them? When they finally met your parents, would they say, "Yes, what wonderful people — your son has told me so many great things about you"?

Jesus rebuked the Pharisees in Matthew 15 for violating the positive duty implied in the fifth commandment. They had developed a tradition by which money dedicated to God (Corban) could not be used to help aging parents. Jesus said: "God commanded, 'Honor your father and mother,' and 'He who curses father or mother, let him be put to death.' But you have made the commandment of God of no effect by your tradition." Both the positive duty (honor them by caring for them) and the negative prohibition (do not curse or dishonor them) are present in this one commandment.

## Does Honor Always Mean Obey?

Obedience is part of honor, but not all of it, and it's not always applicable. The question is: are you still in your parents' household? Are you still dependent on your parents?

For children still in the home: yes, honor means obey. Paul in Ephesians 6:1 says specifically to children, "Obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right." He then quotes the fifth commandment to support this. Note Paul's distinct emphasis on children.

For adult children who have left the home and started their own household: the leave-and-cleave principle of Genesis 2:24 — "Therefore a man shall leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife, and they shall become one flesh" — establishes a new household authority. Once you have moved out and started your own home, you are no longer under your parents' authority in the same way. You are still bound to honor them — deeply and gladly — but not obligated to obey their every instruction as a command.

This also has a specific application for married daughters: you are now under the authority of your husband, not primarily under the authority of your father. Your father is to be deeply honored — but he is not the head of your home. Your husband is. This is an important safeguard against the unhealthy pattern of a wife continuing to look to her parents for direction rather than her husband.

And it cuts the other direction for parents of adult children: you no longer have the right to command your adult children. You may request; you may ask; and I hope they will respond with a disposition of willing service. But it is a request, not a command.

For children still in the home — are there ever exceptions to obedience? Two that are relatively clear: if your parents ask you to sin, you don't obey. And in extreme circumstances, to preserve life. I'll note: if you are currently in your parents' home and find yourself mentally searching for loopholes, go back to the first D — disposition. Is your heart desire to obey, or to find a way around what your parents have asked? Is your desire to honor, or to find the exception that lets you do what you wanted anyway?

## Parents' Obligations

Parents have obligations implied in this commandment too. Two worth noting:

First, hold your children accountable for this commandment. I have watched many sacrificial, loving, caring parents fail in this one area. They can take it when their children dishonor them — they're secure, they're adults. But parents, you are doing your children a major disservice if you do not hold them accountable for how they speak to you and act toward you. The home is the training ground for the rest of life. If they don't learn to honor authority at home, they will carry that deficit into the world.

Second, make it easy for your children to honor you. Chad Van Dixhorn, drawing on William Gooch the Puritan, observes that in Ephesians 5 all the household duties are listed together, not in separate sections — so everyone can read everyone else's duties. Part of the reason is that one way to love others is to make it easier for them to fulfill their obligations. Wives, one way to love your husbands is to make it easy for them to love you. Husbands, one way to love your wives is to make it easy for them to respect you. Parents, one way to love your children is to make it easy for them to honor you. Live in such a way that honor flows naturally from them.

*Closing prayer.*
